name: etched helmet
shortDescription: An etched helmet marked with careful lines, warding curls, or veteran flourishes cut into the metal by a patient hand. It offers protection first, but the decoration tells onlookers this wearer expected to be seen surviving.
flavorText: “Etching turns a helmet from equipment into evidence of pride.”

name: golden grill helmet
shortDescription: A golden grill helmet hides the face behind bars bright enough to catch torchlight across a courtyard. It is defensive, theatrical, and clearly made for someone who wants fear to arrive before recognition.
flavorText: “A gold grill says the wearer can afford both protection and witnesses.”

name: golden winged helmet with gem
shortDescription: A golden winged helmet set with a gem, more ceremonial thunderbolt than ordinary soldier’s gear. It belongs to champions, court guards, or commanders who believe morale improves when armor can be seen from the back row.
flavorText: “Some helmets protect the skull; this one protects the legend.”

name: green helmet
shortDescription: A green helmet painted or enameled for forest troops, household colors, or anyone tired of plain iron. The color softens its shape, but not the purpose beneath it: a hard shell for a harder day.
flavorText: “Green paint does not make war gentle; it only gives it livery.”

name: helmet with flair
shortDescription: A helmet with flair is trimmed, crested, or cut with just enough style to suggest the owner fears anonymity more than arrows. It may still serve in battle, but it was clearly designed to survive gossip as well.
flavorText: “Flair is what armor wears when it expects applause.”

name: helmet with mask
shortDescription: A masked helmet turns the wearer into a shape instead of a person, hiding fear, rank, injury, or intention behind cold metal. It is favored by guards who want obedience and duelists who prefer not to let their eyes negotiate.
flavorText: “A mask makes every silence sound deliberate.”

name: Crownfall commander helmet
shortDescription: A Crownfall commander helmet built with high lines, polished authority, and enough formal weight to make even retreat look organized. It is less about hiding the head than announcing where orders are supposed to begin.
flavorText: “On Crownfall fields, soldiers looked for this helmet before looking for courage.”

name: iron grilled helmet
shortDescription: An iron grilled helmet with a practical face guard, made for impacts that arrive too fast for beauty to matter. Its bars are plain, strong, and easy to trust, which is more than can be said for most battlefield promises.
flavorText: “Iron bars over the face are not elegant, but neither is losing teeth.”

name: plumed helmet
shortDescription: A plumed helmet is crowned with bright feathers or horsehair, as much a banner as a piece of armor. It gives the wearer the dangerous confidence of someone expected to be followed into loud places.
flavorText: “Soldiers protect their heads. Officers decorate them.”

name: plumed helmet with grill
shortDescription: A plumed helmet with a grill mixes parade-ground pride with the sensible fear of facial injury. It lets a commander look splendid from afar and remain mostly recognizable after the fighting reaches arm’s length.
flavorText: “The plume is for the troops; the grill is for reality.”

name: spiked helmet
shortDescription: A spiked helmet adds a rude upward threat to already unfriendly steel. Whether the spike is ceremonial or practical depends on the day, but it certainly improves the wearer’s ability to seem like poor company.
flavorText: “A spike on a helmet is a warning written in silhouette.”

name: standing wings helmet
shortDescription: A standing wings helmet rises dramatically from both sides, giving the wearer the outline of a heraldic beast or ambitious bird. It is terrible in tight doorways and magnificent in paintings, which explains its enduring market.
flavorText: “Winged helmets are proof that vanity has never feared low ceilings.”

name: tall helmet
shortDescription: A tall helmet stretches the head’s outline into something sterner and more formal than ordinary war gear. It is useful for rank, intimidation, and finding your officer in a crowd, provided the crowd is not full of archers.
flavorText: “Height is not command, but it helps command be found.”

name: winged helmet
shortDescription: A winged helmet carries old heroic symbolism in bright metal, suggesting speed, favor, or a family that pays well for myths. It protects like a helmet and advertises like a song.
flavorText: “A winged helmet makes even standing still look like arrival.”

name: banded mail
shortDescription: Banded mail is armor of overlapping metal strips, flexible enough for marching and sturdy enough to make a blade regret certainty. It is not glamorous, but soldiers trust the sort of protection that moves when they do.
flavorText: “Good mail is a compromise between iron and breath.”

name: basic chain mail
shortDescription: Basic chain mail is honest linked protection: heavy on the shoulders, noisy in the pack, and deeply comforting when trouble starts looking for skin. It lacks ornament, which is why veterans often prefer it.
flavorText: “Chain mail sings badly, but it knows the words to survival.”

name: Crownfall general platemail
shortDescription: Crownfall general platemail is broad, polished, and built for an officer expected to be visible through dust, banners, and bad news. Every plate suggests discipline, supply, and the expensive confidence of an army that planned ahead.
flavorText: “A general’s armor is partly steel and partly rumor control.”

name: rough leather armor
shortDescription: Rough leather armor is patched, stiff, and plain enough to tell the truth about its owner’s purse. It will not impress a court armorer, but it has protected plenty of ribs in roadside fights where elegance never arrived.
flavorText: “Rough leather is what courage wears before it can afford iron.”

name: colorful scale mail
shortDescription: Colorful scale mail layers small plates into a bright, fishlike shimmer, turning protection into a moving festival of color. It is favored by guards, duelists, and anyone who wants rivals to remember who ruined their day.
flavorText: “Scale mail need not be dull just because the work is serious.”

name: dwarven ringmail
shortDescription: Dwarven ringmail is dense, orderly, and made with the patient stubbornness of smiths who consider shortcuts a personal insult. It sits heavy, lasts long, and has the reassuring feel of armor built for grandchildren.
flavorText: “Dwarven mail is not worn in; the wearer is.”

name: starcourt leather armor
shortDescription: Starcourt leather armor is supple, dark, and finished with celestial trims that make practical gear look slightly enchanted. It suits scouts, courtiers, and night guards who need to move quietly without appearing poorly dressed.
flavorText: “At Starcourt, even leather is expected to know the constellations.”

name: starcourt ringmail
shortDescription: Starcourt ringmail joins sturdy metal rings with a courtly sense of pattern, making defense look measured rather than brutal. It belongs to guards who must stand under chandeliers as often as open skies.
flavorText: “Starcourt armor must survive battle and still pass inspection by candlelight.”

name: starcourt scalemail
shortDescription: Starcourt scalemail overlaps polished plates like a night sky made obedient to armorers. It catches light in small disciplined flashes, enough to look rich without surrendering the hard purpose underneath.
flavorText: “A Starcourt scale is a tiny mirror taught to stop knives.”

name: faerie platemail
shortDescription: Faerie platemail is too light for its shine and too beautiful to trust completely. The plates seem grown as much as forged, making mortal smiths admire it with the exact expression they use for unfairness.
flavorText: “Faerie armor weighs less than steel and more than a promise.”

name: banded mail
shortDescription: This finer banded mail is tighter in its joins and cleaner in its finish, the sort made for buyers who expect inspection before combat. It carries the same dependable principle as common banding, only with fewer excuses hidden under the straps.
flavorText: “Fine banded mail proves plain armor can still have standards.”
